data.title: Apple praised by Needham analyst over CEO switch; firm pounds the table on Disney deal
data.description:
Following the surprising timing of Apple (AAPL) naming John Ternus as its next CEO, succeeding Tim Cook, a number of Wall Street analysts and investors have praised the tech giant for the move.

Count Needham analyst Laura Martin among those championing the decision, even as she repeatedly pushed Apple to aggressively move away from its core ethos and delve deeper into advertising and M&A.

“We are supportive of the succession plan announced yesterday by AAPL, whereby Tim Cook will leave as CEO on Sept. 1 (to become Executive Chairman of the Board), and John Ternus, currently SVP of Hardware Engineering, will become the CEO of AAPL,” Martin wrote in a note to clients. “We are optimistic about value creation from this transition because we believe Ternus will bring a sense of urgency (i.e., speed) and risk-taking (i.e., innovation) to AAPL's product line.”

Martin, who has a rare Hold rating on Apple, continued to pound the table that Apple move further into advertising. She went so far as to say that she believes the company “has been destroying shareholder value by underexploiting high-margin advertising [revenues].”

In her estimate, Apple generated roughly $10B in ad revenue in 2025, less than 10% of revenue from its Services segment and less than 3% of total revenue. “We believe this revenue stream should be closer to ~50% of Services revs and, at ~80% margins, a key upside profit driver for AAPL,” Martin added.

Last month, Apple opened up its near-ubiquitous Maps app to advertising and recently expanded advertising inside the App Store.

Martin also said that Apple needs to buy (or at least partner with) media giant Walt Disney (DIS), a deal she has repeatedly championed to no avail.

“We believe AAPL should partner with, or buy, Disney, in order to drive longer engagement lengths and give it differentiated assets (i.e., films and TV series) that have pricing power and powerful moats,” Martin explained. “We believe AAPL should also be using M&A, partnerships, and industry leadership to accelerate value creation.”

The two companies have a long-standing relationship. Apple co-founder Steve Jobs became the largest Disney shareholder after he sold Pixar to Disney in 2006.

Apple shares fell 2.5% in midday trading on Tuesday.

data.title: AT&T, Verizon Q1 earnings on deck: Focus on subscribers, FCF numbers
data.description:
AT&T (T) is set to post quarterly results on Wednesday, and investors will focus on fiber and wireless phone subscribers, along with free cash flow for the U.S. carrier.

Wall Street expects the company to post EPS of $0.55, implying a rise of nearly 8%, while revenue is expected to be $31.24 billion during the quarter.

Starting this first quarter, AT&T will report under three segments, namely “Advanced Connectivity,” “Legacy,” and "Latin America." The advanced connectivity segment will cover domestic 5G and fiber services, while legacy will ‍include copper-based voice and data services, and Latin America represents Mexico wireless operations.

Texas-based AT&T has put its bet on billion dollar deals to expand its wireless and fiber networks and stay ahead of rivals by luring customers with faster internet speeds and better mobile coverage. The company’s nearly $6B deal to acquire Lumen’s (LUMN) consumer fiber business and a $23 billion acquisition of EchoStar's (SATS) spectrum licence got positive reactions from analysts and investors.

The company, in January, reported a strong top- and bottom-line beat for the fourth quarter and provided a full-year profit outlook that was above expectations.  FCF is expected to be more than $18B in 2026, $19B+ in 2027, and $21B+ in 2028, AT&T said.

With the company reporting under new segment, Seeking Alpha analyst Louis Gerard said he “will want to see how the firm presents the integration of Lumen and whether these two new segments provide a greater view into fiber economics.”

“This has historically been a point of frustration for investors, as it has been difficult to isolate the high-growth fiber and wireless businesses from the legacy copper and business wireline operations. If this satisfies what we have wanted for a while now, I believe that the firm could see a better valuation multiple on the market,” Gerard noted, adding that the debt balance of the company is also worth looking at during earnings.

Over the last two years, AT&T has beaten both revenue and EPS estimates 63% of the time.

Over the last three months, EPS estimates have seen seven upward revisions, compared to two downward revisions. Revenue estimates have been revised upwards six times versus two downward moves.

Rival Verizon (VZ) is also set to post quarterly earnings on April 27, with Wall Street expecting the company to post EPS of $1.21, on revenue of $34.86 billion, implying a rise of 4% during the quarter.

New York-based Verizon provided a robust outlook for the full year in January boosted by strong additions for postpaid phone subscribers.

Over the last two years, Verizon has beaten EPS estimates 88% of the time and has beaten revenue estimates 63% of the time.

A recent Seeking Alpha analysis from Juxtaposed Ideas said Verizon “demonstrates excellent capital allocation across efficient growth capex, accretive M&A, secure dividend payouts, and new $25B share repurchase authorization through 2028.” However, the analyst cautioned of a seasonally weaker FCF generation in the first quarter. 

Over the last three months, Verizon EPS estimates have seen seven upward revisions and three downward revisions. Revenue estimates have seen eight upward revisions versus no downward moves.

Seeking Alpha analysts and Wall Street are bullish and rated both the stocks a Buy. In contrast, Seeking Alpha’s Quant ratings are cautious and consider them a Hold.

AT&T has gained nearly 5% so far this year, while Verizon jumped 14%. This is in comparison to the nearly 4% rise in the broader S&P 500 Index.

data.title: Philip Morris Q1 earnings on deck: What to expect
data.description:
Philip Morris International (PM) will report its results for the first quarter on Wednesday, before markets open.

Wall Street expects the company to post earnings of $1.83 per share, implying a rise of around 8%, on revenue of $9.91B, representing year-over-year growth of about 7%.

During the quarter, Philip Morris remained focused on advancing its shift away from traditional cigarettes, highlighting progress in its smoke-free product portfolio and outlining plans to strengthen its long-term positioning. The company raised its profit outlook while pointing to continued growth in smoke-free revenues and reiterated its expectations for mid-single-digit organic revenue growth.

It also announced plans to expand its operational footprint, including launching a new solutions center in Florida and increasing U.S.-focused investments, while navigating regulatory challenges such as India maintaining its e-cigarette ban. Separately, Philip Morris sought regulatory support  for its ZYN product in the U.S.

According to Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating system, PM is rated Buy with an overall score of 4.01 out of 5, reflecting an A+ grade in terms of profitability, but it has a C- in terms of valuation.

A recent Seeking Alpha analysis said Philip Morris International could see an upside surprise in its first-quarter 2026 results, citing the stock’s tendency to react sharply on earnings and estimates pointing to stronger adjusted earnings. The analysis noted that growth in the company’s smoke-free business could support performance, adding that “the adjusted EPS could surprise upwards, which can be positive for price.”

Over the past two years, PM has beaten EPS estimates 100% of the time and has beaten revenue estimates 75% of the time.

Over the last three months, EPS estimates have seen no upward revisions and six downward revisions, while revenue estimates have seen no upward revisions and six downward revisons.

data.title: Kalshi plans to offer crypto trading through perpetuals - report
data.description:
Prediction markets platform Kalshi plans to offer crypto trading through perpetual futures, according to a media report on Tuesday.

The Information reported the news, citing people familiar with the matter. Perpetual futures, which have no expiration date, are a kind of leveraged product that's popular on the Binance and Hyperliquid platforms.

The company may expand perpetuals to other assets in the future, according to the article.

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) recently traded at $75.8K, edging down 0.1% over the past 24 hours. Ethereum (ETH-USD), off 0.3%, recently traded at ~$2.31K.

data.title: Shell faces new court case seeking ban on new oil and gas developments
data.description:
A Dutch climate activist group launched a new ​court case against Shell (SHEL) on Tuesday in the Netherlands, seeking to prohibit the company from developing any new oil and gas projects, Reuters reported.

"Shell must stop bringing new oil and gas ​fields into production. This is necessary to protect us from Shell's climate-disrupting activities," Friends of the Earth ​Netherlands said in the summons it filed against the company.

Shell (SHEL) called the case "unreasonable" after Friends of the Earth announced plans to file the legal action last year, saying the activist group ignored the global economy's need for ​fossil fuels and production would simply shift to other companies if Shell were ordered to halt ​operations.

In 2024, the Court of Appeal of The Hague awarded Shell (SHEL) with a victory in a landmark climate case, overturning a lower court ruling that had obliged the company to slash its greenhouse gas emissions.

Like many of its peers, Shell (SHEL) has scaled back its ​renewable energy operations and lowered emissions targets, and the company told investors last year that it was targeting a 4%-5% annual increase in liquefied natural gas sales over the next five years.

A date for a ​hearing in the new case has not been set yet.

data.title: RTX raises 2026 adjusted EPS outlook to $6.70-$6.90 while projecting $92.5B-$93.5B in sales
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: RTX Corporation (RTX) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We delivered very strong performance to start the year" (Chairman & CEO Christopher Calio). RTX reported "adjusted sales were $22.1 billion" and "adjusted EPS of $1.78," alongside "free cash flow of $1.3 billion" (Chairman & CEO Calio).

  • "Our book-to-bill in the quarter was 1.14, and our backlog is a record $271 billion" (Chairman & CEO Calio). Calio highlighted defense awards including "over $3 billion for F135 lot 19 production" at Pratt, Collins awards "close to $3 billion," and Raytheon awards of "$6.6 billion" including Patriot and LTAMDS-related wins (Chairman & CEO Calio).

  • "As we previously announced, Raytheon signed 5 landmark framework agreements" for critical munitions, and "once finalized, these agreements would provide firm demand signals" over "the next decade" (Chairman & CEO Calio). He said RTX has already "invested nearly $900 million in CapEx over the last 3 years" at key sites and expects to make "significant additional investments going forward" (Chairman & CEO Calio).

  • "We are updating our full year outlook" (Executive VP & CFO Neil Mitchill). Mitchill said RTX is raising adjusted sales outlook to "$92.5 billion to $93.5 billion" and adjusted EPS to "$6.70" to "$6.90," while keeping free cash flow outlook at "$8.25 billion" to "$8.75 billion" (Executive VP & CFO Mitchill).

Outlook

  • "We’re raising our full year outlook for adjusted sales and EPS and maintaining our free cash flow outlook" (Chairman & CEO Calio). Management attributed the raise to "continued defense strength" and "the performance we saw at Raytheon" (Executive VP & CFO Mitchill).

  • Mitchill raised expected defense sales growth to "mid- to high single digits" from "mid-single digits," while keeping commercial expectations: "commercial OE sales to grow mid-single digits" and "commercial aftermarket sales to grow high single digits" (Executive VP & CFO Mitchill).

  • Versus the prior quarter’s initial 2026 guide of $92B-$93B sales and $6.60-$6.80 adjusted EPS, Q1 guidance language shifted to an explicit increase: "raising our full-year adjusted sales outlook by $500 million" and increasing EPS by "$0.10" on both ends (Executive VP & CFO Mitchill).

Financial Results

  • RTX reported Q1 "adjusted sales of $22.1 billion" and said organic growth was driven by "commercial OE up 6%, commercial aftermarket up 14% and defense up 9%" (Executive VP & CFO Mitchill). Mitchill also said Q1 adjusted segment operating profit was "$2.9 billion" and adjusted EPS was "$1.78" (Executive VP & CFO Mitchill).

  • Mitchill cited profitability and cost themes: "70 basis points of consolidated segment margin expansion" and "only a 1% increase in head count" (Executive VP & CFO Mitchill). He said adjusted EPS benefited by "about $0.08" from a lower effective tax rate and noted "lower interest expense" (Executive VP & CFO Mitchill).

  • Segment details included Collins Q1 sales of "$7.6 billion" and Pratt Q1 sales of "$8.2 billion," while Raytheon Q1 sales were "$6.9 billion" with operating profit of "$845 million" (Vice President of Investor Relations Nathan Ware). Ware said Raytheon bookings were "$6.6 billion" with book-to-bill "0.96" and backlog of "$74 billion" (Vice President Ware).

Q&A

  • Robert Stallard, Vertical Research Partners, LLC: asked about supply chain capacity and rare earth risk for missile demand; Chairman & CEO Calio answered "munitions output was up over 40% year-over-year," said the framework agreements could create "long-term firm demand" that enables suppliers to invest, and added RTX is covered "in the near and medium term" on critical minerals.

  • Peter Arment, Baird: pressed on framework-agreement pricing, CapEx, and margins; Chairman & CEO Calio said RTX has been investing already and that the agreements can enable "economy of scale" and "production efficiencies," while noting negotiations are ongoing.

  • Myles Walton, Wolfe Research: asked about effector and sensor growth; Executive VP & CFO Mitchill said effectors are "a little bit over 40%" of Raytheon sales and said "we’re seeing double-digit growth rates" in both effectors and sensors, with sensors tied to Patriot, NASAMS, Coyote, and LTAMDS.

  • Mariana Perez Mora, BofA: asked about tariff impacts and IEEPA ruling; Executive VP & CFO Mitchill said "no change today to our outlook for tariffs" and disclosed RTX "paid about $500 million" under IEEPA-related tariffs, with refunds not included in guidance.

  • Scott Deuschle, Deutsche Bank: asked Hot Section Plus pricing and GTF Advantage margin effects; Chairman & CEO Calio said RTX plans to "get value for the investment" and that Hot Section Plus enters MRO "later this year." Executive VP & CFO Mitchill said Advantage has higher cost and pricing, with "not a lot of headwind on a per engine basis" and reiterated "a couple of hundred million dollars of headwind on OE margins" this year.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts slightly negative to skeptical, concentrating on supply chain constraints, tariffs/refunds, GTF AOG trajectory, and aftermarket sensitivity to travel; questions included "how concerned are you" (Stallard) and "guidance is even a bit conservative" (Godyn, Citi).

  • Management slightly positive in prepared remarks and more explanatory in Q&A, repeating execution and backlog strength while using caution on unresolved items (e.g., "still in the process of negotiations" on framework agreements) and guidance conservatism ("it’s one quarter" at Raytheon) (Chairman & CEO Calio; Executive VP & CFO Mitchill).

  • Versus the prior quarter’s tone emphasizing strong momentum and initial 2026 guideposts, this call included more event-driven monitoring language: "we’re closely monitoring global events" and "we’re not making any changes to our commercial outlook" despite volatility (Chairman & CEO Calio).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance moved upward in Q1 2026: sales raised to $92.5B-$93.5B from $92B-$93B and adjusted EPS raised to $6.70-$6.90 from $6.60-$6.80, while free cash flow stayed $8.25B-$8.75B (Executive VP & CFO Mitchill).

  • Defense emphasis increased, with Q1 discussion centered on potential decade-long framework agreements and accelerated munitions output, versus Q4’s broader 2026 setup and capital allocation balance (Chairman & CEO Calio).

  • Analyst focus shifted from portfolio/capital allocation and long-term margin targets (Q4) toward near-term execution constraints (supply chain, rare earths, tariffs refunds, GTF AOG visibility) and how quickly Raytheon can sustain Q1 strength (Q1).

Risks and Concerns

  • Supply chain scalability for munitions and constrained inputs were flagged, including "rocket motors" and "microelectronics" risks (Chairman & CEO Calio). Management said framework agreements can improve visibility and help attract "additional suppliers" for resiliency.

  • Tariff uncertainty and refund timing remained open; Mitchill said RTX has not recorded refund-related income and "have not included that in our guidance" (Executive VP & CFO Mitchill).

  • Commercial volatility monitoring was explicit, with Calio citing a "dynamic" environment tied to Middle East disruption but stating RTX is not changing its commercial outlook yet (Chairman & CEO Calio).

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a strong start with $22.1B adjusted sales and a raised full-year outlook to $92.5B-$93.5B and $6.70-$6.90 adjusted EPS, driven primarily by Raytheon volume, mix, and productivity. Leadership emphasized munitions ramp execution (deliveries up over 40% year-over-year) and described framework agreements as a potential long-duration demand signal that could support higher capacity investment, while acknowledging ongoing negotiations, tariff/refund uncertainty, and the need to keep improving GTF fleet health and supply chain throughput.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Fed Chair race in focus: Warsh is grilled and traders signal delay
data.description:
Attention is increasingly focused on the leadership outlook at the Federal Reserve as Kevin Warsh, nominated by President Donald Trump to serve as chair, faces scrutiny during his Senate Banking Committee confirmation hearing.

Lawmakers are expected to press Warsh on key issues including monetary policy independence, inflation dynamics, and the future path of interest rates. 

Meanwhile, market-based indicators suggest growing uncertainty around his nomination. Prediction market data from Kalshi currently implies just a 24% probability that Warsh will be confirmed by May 15, the date marking the end of Jerome Powell’s term. 

The relatively low odds highlight investor skepticism about the timing and likelihood of a leadership transition, even as the nomination process continues to unfold in Washington. 

Below is when traders on Kalshi believe Warsh will be confirmed as Fed Chair: 

  • Before May 1, 2026 — 2.9% chance.
  • Before May 15, 2026 — 24% chance.
  • Before June 1, 2026 — 68% chance.
  • Before July 1, 2026 — 83% chance.
  • Before August 1, 2026 — 90.7% chance.

Market Tracking ETFs: (DIA), (DDM), (DOG), (DXD), (SDOW), (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), (SPXU), (QQQ), (QQQM), (TQQQ), (QID), and (SQQQ).

data.title: Wall Street is lower as a U.S.-Iran truce remains uncertain, Warsh faces confirmation hearing
data.description:
U.S. stocks were in red territory on Tuesday as investors hoped for a peace deal between Iran and the U.S., while former Fed Governor Kevin Warsh started his confirmation hearings in the Senate.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last -0.3%, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -0.3%, and the Dow (DJI) was -0.2%.

Over in the bond market, yields were higher. The benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 5 basis points higher at 4.30%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) rose 6 basis points to 3.79%.

U.S. President Donald Trump told CNBC that he expects the U.S. to “end up with a great deal” with Iran to end the war. He also said he does not expect to extend the ongoing ceasefire, which will expire on Wednesday. “I think they have no choice,” he said.

Vice President JD Vance, along with the U.S. delegation, is expected to land in Islamabad, Pakistan, on Wednesday for another round of talks, CNN reported.

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) were higher at $90, while Brent (CO1:COM) also rose to $98 per barrel.

Kevin Warsh, the former Fed governor whom President Donald Trump nominated as Federal Reserve chair, faced a barrage of questions on Tuesday during his confirmation hearings in the Senate. The confirmation hearing started at 10 a.m. ET.

Warsh accused the Fed of "missing the mark" when it failed to rein in inflation after COVID-19 in 2021 and 2022. "We need fundamental policy reforms to fix it," he said.

On the U.S. economy, retail sales jumped more than expected in March.

Pending home sales jumped 1.5% month-over-month in March, higher than consensus, the National Association of Realtors said. Moreover, February business inventories rose +0.4% MoM to $2,686.8B vs. +0.2% consensus, according to the U.S. Census Bureau.

data.title: D.R. Horton expects $33.5B-$34.5B of fiscal 2026 revenue while projecting at least $3B of operating cash flow
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: D.R. Horton (DHI) Q2 fiscal 2026

Management View

  • "The D.R. Horton team delivered solid second quarter results with consolidated pretax income of $867 million on $7.6 billion of revenues and a pretax profit margin of 11.5%." (President, CEO & Director Paul Romanowski)
  • "Our sales incentives increased during the second quarter, and we expect incentives to remain elevated for the rest of the year with a level dependent on demand, mortgage interest rates and other market conditions." (President, CEO & Director Romanowski)
  • "Earnings for the second quarter of fiscal 2026 were $2.24 per diluted share compared to $2.58 per share in the prior year quarter." (Executive VP & COO Michael Murray)
  • "Net sales orders increased 11% year-over-year in the second quarter to 24,992 homes, while total order value increased 10% to $9.2 billion, in line with our business plan and expectations." (Executive VP & CFO Bill Wheat)

Outlook

  • "Looking ahead to the third quarter, we currently expect consolidated revenues to be in the range of $8.8 billion to $9.3 billion, with homes closed by our homebuilding operations to be in the range of 23,500 to 24,000 homes." (Senior VP of Communications & People and Head of Investor Relations Jessica Hansen)
  • "For the full year of fiscal 2026, we now expect consolidated revenues of approximately $33.5 billion to $34.5 billion and homes closed by our homebuilding operations of 86,000 to 87,500 homes." (Senior VP Hansen)
  • "We continue to forecast an income tax rate for fiscal 2026 of approximately 24.5%, operating cash flow of at least $3 billion, common stock repurchases of approximately $2.5 billion and dividend payments of around $500 million." (Senior VP Hansen)
  • "We lowered our closings guide by 500." (Senior VP Hansen)

Financial Results

  • "Our gross profit margin on home sales revenue in the second quarter was 20.1%, which included a 40 basis point benefit from a favorable litigation outcome and lower-than-normal warranty costs." (Senior VP Hansen)
  • "Home sales revenues in the second quarter totaled $7 billion on 19,486 homes closed" and "Our average closing price was $361,600, down 1% sequentially and down 3% year-over-year." (Executive VP & COO Murray)
  • "During the second quarter, we repurchased 6 million shares of common stock for $904 million, reducing our outstanding share count by 8% compared to a year ago." (Executive VP & CFO Wheat)
  • "In the second quarter, our rental operations generated $12 million of pretax income on $212 million of revenues" and "Pretax income for the second quarter was $52 million on $193 million of revenues, resulting in a pretax profit margin of 26.8%." (President, CEO & Director Romanowski)

Q&A

  • Alan Ratner, Zelman & Associates LLC: "I would love to drill in a little bit more on the gross margin outlook." Executive VP & CFO Wheat: "we expect to see some incremental benefits in Q3 and Q4" and "Right now, we have nothing tangible to report in terms of anticipating inflation" from oil.
  • John Lovallo, UBS: "how would you sort of characterize demand in March relative to normal seasonality?" Executive VP & COO Murray: "We saw sales in line with normal seasonality" and "we're pleased with our sales results through mid-April."
  • Stephen Kim, Evercore ISI: "could you give us a sense for overall, what incentives may, in aggregate, represent" as a percent? President, CEO & Director Romanowski: "the incentives as a percent of revs is roughly 10%."
  • Trevor Allinson, Wolfe Research: "Have you seen the industry overall also start to make some progress on working down inventory?" President, CEO & Director Romanowski: "I think we have seen inventory levels reduce across the competitive environment."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly positive, with repeated acknowledgement of execution in a difficult demand backdrop (e.g., "strong results here in a tough market" (Stephen Kim, Evercore ISI)).
  • Management: neutral-to-slightly positive, emphasizing discipline and conditionality around demand and rates (e.g., "we expect incentives to remain elevated" (President, CEO & Director Romanowski) and "we will continue to adjust to market conditions with discipline" (President, CEO & Director Romanowski)).
  • Versus last quarter: the prepared tone stayed consistent on affordability and incentives; Q2 Q&A added more focus on oil/material inflation timing and on rental-policy uncertainty.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • "Net sales orders increased 11%" in Q2 vs. "net sales orders increased 3%" in Q1. (Executive VP & CFO Wheat; Executive VP & CFO Wheat)
  • Q2 inventory commentary emphasized faster turns and fewer specs: "Our completed unsold homes are down 25% from December and 35% from a year ago" vs. Q1: "7,300 of our unsold homes at quarter end were completed." (President, CEO & Director Romanowski)
  • Full-year guide language tightened on the high end: Q2: "$33.5 billion to $34.5 billion" and "86,000 to 87,500" vs. Q1: "$33.5 billion to $35 billion" and "86,000 to 88,000." (Senior VP Hansen)

Risks and Concerns

  • "New home demand remains impacted by affordability constraints and cautious consumer sentiment." (President, CEO & Director Romanowski)
  • On build-for-rent policy uncertainty: "there is uncertainty around the legislation" and "a little bit of a pause" in activity. (President, CEO & Director Romanowski)
  • On cost risk: "If we were to see an extended period where oil prices stayed elevated for an extended period, then there could be some pressure." (Executive VP & CFO Wheat)

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q2 as a quarter where sales and margins held up better than expected amid affordability pressure, while reiterating that incentives should remain elevated and results will stay rate- and demand-dependent. Guidance for fiscal 2026 was narrowed on the high end, with the company continuing to target at least $3 billion in operating cash flow and approximately $2.5 billion in buybacks, alongside ongoing efforts to improve cycle times, reduce completed inventory, and protect returns through disciplined starts and lot underwriting.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Halliburton rises after Q1 earnings topper; sees oil recovery coming in North America
data.description:
Halliburton (HAL) up 4% in Tuesday's trading after reporting better-than-expected Q1 adjusted earnings, as strong revenue growth in Latin America helped offset weaker sales due to the Middle East war.

The company's Q1 revenue for Latin America jumped 22% Y/Y to $1.1B, driven by higher activity in Ecuador, the Caribbean, and Brazil, and revenue for Europe and Africa rose 11% to $858M.

Steady demand in those regions helped offset a 13% decline in revenue for the Middle East and Asia, as attacks on energy infrastructure in countries such as Saudi Arabia and Qatar have dragged down crude production levels across the region.

The war reduced Q1 net income in Halliburton's (HAL) drilling and evaluation divisions by $0.02-$0.03/share, with impacts in the $0.07-$0.09 range for Q2, CFO Eric Carre said on the company's earnings conference call.

Q1 revenue in North America fell 4.5% to $2.14B, but CEO Jeff Miller said the company is in the "early innings of a recovery."

"We are increasingly bullish ⁠on ​the [North America] recovery as the back end of the ​oil curve picks up to $80-plus," Barclays analysts said in a note.

Halliburton (HAL) "posted a solid beat across the board... driven by international strength that more than offset continued North America softness," Melus Research analyst James West said in a note.

Halliburton (HAL) is the first major oilfield services contractor to report results since the start of the war, which has been a blow to the sector that looks to the Middle East for growth as shale fields in the U.S. mature.
